Well, he's absolutely got a major problem with the fact that when he ran for office the very first time, as the state treasurer or as a candidate for treasurer, he said: Trust me, Im a banker, I know what Im doing.

So thats the Senate race.
In the race for governor, who's running?

In the race for governor, we have an incumbent, Pat Quinn, who was the two-time running mate of now-convicted former Governor Rod Blagojevich, who is trying hard to tell the Illinois voters that he is a different kind of governor than the guy he shared a state house with for six years.

And he's opposed by a veteran Republican state senator, Bill Brady, from central Illinois, who is not very well-known to most voters in Illinois but has benefited in the early going by the idea that he is not Pat Quinn.
In truth, as lieutenant governor, Quinn actually was fairly independent, politically, of Blagojevich, wasn't he?
They run separately in primaries in Illinois.
That's right, they do.
They have to run together on a ticket in the fall campaign, in the general election.
And so the Republicans right now are plastering billboards all over town showing Pat Quinn standing up for Rod Blagojevich.
But the truth is, at the end of the Blagojevich administration, Quinn was out there telling people: I don't approve of the fundraising tear that this guy's on.
He's not doing the right things.
He opposed him on taxes and a number of other issues.
So he can fairly claim that he was independent.
But the Republicans are going to keep trying to say that he ran as part of the Quinn-Blagojevich years.
Jim Webb of the Chicago Tribune, thanks for talking with us.
Thank you, good to be here.

Throughout this tour, the Axis of Evil trio has been holding auditions, looking for local talent.
Not all the tryouts went smoothly.
